transcript.transcript: Alright, so, you know, we realize this is kind of a long shot, but part of our motivation was to embarrass the state, you know, by suing them, by getting publicity, and by, you know, getting our case even better to the public, which would give us additional leverage in a legislative process. And, you know, at the time, Judge Beitman was still in the Supreme Court, and we had a, you know, a shot at getting a 3-2. McDevitt and Johnson were, you know, Democratic appointees. So, you know, we had some shots, but we knew all along that the legislative process was where we really didn't have a chance. So the idea was to just kind of forego it for right then and see what happened in 96? Right. Okay. Okay. The final law, was it the law that you wanted? Was it all-inclusive of everything that you everything that you thought you would need? Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, it was an entirely clean bill. It provided no exceptions for small ag employers. It just deleted the exemption, so farm workers are treated like everybody else. And, you know, and so in retrospect, you know, we were fortunate that that 95 compromise bill failed, and the ag employer would have been better served to live up to their deal, and would have gotten a better deal. But that strategy failed them in the end. And so we've got a law that's, you know, better than a lot of states. Better than Texas's law. Texas has the exemption for 10 employees. But a lot of states treat farm workers like everyone else. California, Washington, Washington, Oregon. And that's what we were shooting for. And that's what we got. Okay. Okay. As far as the whole issue of workers' compensation in Idaho, was there a feeling going going around? Is this the time for this? Or what was the main motivation of the state in general in general to handle this issue? What kind of causes come with this? Right. this? Right. Well, you know, like I said, with that first bill that wasn't printed in 93, at that point, there wasn't any push behind it. There wasn't any publicity. And so, you know, that was our that was our first job, was to bring attention to it. And we really succeeded in 94. Like I said, two or three days of hearings before a House State Affairs Committee. But, you know, the big difference in 96 was, you know, the governor's support, the work of the Idaho statesmen, and in particular Michelle Cole and Editor Bob Costa. Michelle worked really hard on it. They went after every angle. They really worked hard on it. And I think, you know, I mean, we deserve some credit there, too, because we found the people for them to talk to. We provided interpreters. You know, we took them places. We gave them the information. And they followed up on pretty much every angle that we thought was important. Plus, they picked up on a whole bunch of their own. And then, of course, the other big element in in all this was the injury to Mr. Waditz, the guy who lost his arms and one leg in the terrible accident down in Malta, right? And the statesmen just followed that very closely. And that really opened up eyes to the public and made it very difficult for the legislators to oppose it any longer. So that was just immensely important. Anything Anything maybe I haven't asked you about this whole process or any insight maybe that you have as that you have as far as things I haven't asked you about this whole process? Yeah. There's one other thing that's pretty important asked you about this whole process? Yeah. There's one other thing that's pretty important is, you know, to get something done in Idaho, you've got to work with Republicans. And, you know, there were some folks that really did a great job on this. And Bill Deal probably is the main one. Because he started pushing this before there was much support for it. He was on the House State Affairs Committee. He was the vice chair that voted down the bill in 1994. But he is a real principled guy. And to give us something, they worked it out so that they would send it to would send it to an interim committee over the summer. And they got some money to do that. I mean, they mean, they had to find money to be able to fly the legislators in for that committee, you know, and to do the work of that committee. And so that was really meaningful. And like I said, I said, as it turned out, that interim committee was what turned the tide. And he was on that interim committee. And, you know, at first he wasn't completely sold on this. He's an insurance agent. But I think as soon as he got all the information, he saw that it was the was the right thing to do. And eventually he became our strongest advocate. And he pushed real hard amongst his colleagues to get it done. He took the brunt of it. He got a lot of criticism, I I know, locally in Cannon County. He's from Tampa, for his support of the bill. And I think even at one point he was afraid that he might get voted out because of it. Although it turns out that he just won by an overwhelming margin the last time he ran. I guess probably 96. And it's because he really does represent the best of what a statesman can be. And he deserves he deserves a lot of credit for it. He got a lot of work done. And there was others, like Dean like Dean Cameron did a nice piece in 96, and of course the governor. And Senator Carrick from Caldwell as well. He pushed the bill on the Senate side. He was the one who introduced it on the Senate side. And before the Senate committee and before on the Senate floor, he kind of made his spiel. And he had a lot of, he's not in the Senate anymore, but he had he had a lot of clout and respect. And it's because those Republicans came to the forefront to do the right thing. Even though a lot of their constituency was adamantly opposed to it. to it. That made a huge difference. And those folks deserve a lot of credit, especially Bill Bill Deal. Great. Final Deal. Great. Final question here. If it hadn't passed as of right now, say a year later, what would you think it would be right now if it hadn't passed? If it hadn't passed if it hadn't passed? If it hadn't passed in 96? Well, you know, it's really hard to say. I'm pretty sure we would have filed the suit. Hard to say where that would have helped or hurt. People often respond negatively to that. It might have just hopped the ante just a little bit more, which might have got it done. On the other hand, if we got that close and failed, a lot of of people might have backed off, including the governor, and we might have been dead. I don't know. I'm not that politically savvy. I don't know what would have happened. 96 is just a year where everything came together. Let me add one more thing, because I think it really is important that the credit goes to the people that deserve it. I've talked about the governor and Juarez and the Republican legislators. Certainly I've talked about what me and Ernie have done, Jack Barrett, John Greenfield. But a huge part of this was when this was getting no attention, we had kind of this little grassroots thing going. We had rallies in the winter of 94, before the legislature started, and in the winter of 95, so probably December or January, somewhere around there. I can look at my old flyers. We had rallies at Julia Davis Park, and then we marched to the Capitol. We had letter-writing campaigns. Farm workers came from Caldwell and Holmdale and Wilder, and even from central Idaho. I forget the numbers, but we had several hundred people show up to these things. That made for great publicity, and it started to turn the heat up. There were individuals like Delia Gonzalez, an outreach worker at Idaho Legal Aid, and Martha Torres, who was an outreach worker at the Idaho Department of Labor, who went door-to-door at farm labor camps, telling people about the rally and getting people to show up. There was a lot of that kind of work going on, too, that was really important in getting the ball rolling. Who organized that? It was under the auspices of the Farm Work Resource Committee. At the time, the folks most involved were myself, Phil Bowman, who was in research and analysis at the Department of Labor, who was a Department of Employment Admin. He was a chair of the Farm Work Resource Committee, and then I became a chair somewhere in the middle, around 94, 95. Phil and I worked really closely on that. For a while, the Idaho Migrant Council was really involved. I shouldn't say involved. I shouldn't say for a while, they were pretty involved the whole way through. We just kind of of all pooled our resources, all the farm worker advocates, to get these rallies going, get the letter writing campaigns done, to get a lot of the information that the legislators needed, and to provide testimony at the various hearings. The Farm Work Resource Committee deserves a lot of credit for that, and the several individuals that did the bulk of that work through the FRC. Oh, and I would be remiss not to say more about Jesse Berane. I probably already talked to to you about Jesse. Jesse, of course, another Republican legislator who had a huge impact on this process. He's very close with the governor, and he did a lot of the inside work in getting things going. Like I said, he was a co-sponsor of the bill in 95, no, not 96, because he wasn't in the legislature in 96, but worked very hard behind the scenes, and was kind of like the go-between between the farm worker advocates and the governor, and always gave long, impassioned speeches about how unfair it was to the farm workers, because Jesse was a farm worker, and had lived through all the sorts of discrimination that farm workers have faced over the years in Idaho. Where's Where's he from? Let's Let's see. Where is Jesse from? I think he lives now in Boise, I believe. Yeah, he lives in Boise in Boise, I believe. Yeah, he lives in Boise now, but he's been in Idaho for 40 years. Probably from Texas initially, but he would tell the story about when he first came to Idaho, there would be signs in the stores that would say, no Mexicans or dogs allowed. He goes way back to the 40s or 50s. He's not doing farm work in Idaho. So Jesse's a good guy to talk to. Well, we're good. Anything else you want to get on tape for the state archives right now? I really right now? I really appreciate your effort, because like I said, we're going to forget all this stuff. We already are forgetting it. Thank you.

lines.line: > The initial strategy involved suing the state to gain publicity and leverage in the legislative process. The aim was to improve public perception and influence legislative changes, despite the slim chances in the legislative process.
** strategy, publicity, lawsuit, legislative process, leverage **
keywords: ** strategy, publicity, lawsuit, legislative process, leverage **

metadata.title: Motivation and Initial Strategy
metadata.synopsis: The initial strategy involved suing the state to gain publicity and leverage in the legislative process. The aim was to improve public perception and influence legislative changes, despite the slim chances in the legislative process.
metadata.keywords: strategy, publicity, lawsuit, legislative process, leverage

lines.line: # The Final Law and Its Impact

> The final law successfully treated farm workers like other employees, removing exemptions for small agricultural employers. The 95 compromise bill's failure turned into a strategic advantage, surpassing the worker compensation laws of many other states.
** final law, farm workers, exemptions, legislative success, worker compensation **
keywords: ** final law, farm workers, exemptions, legislative success, worker compensation **

metadata.title: The Final Law and Its Impact
metadata.synopsis: The final law successfully treated farm workers like other employees, removing exemptions for small agricultural employers. The 95 compromise bill's failure turned into a strategic advantage, surpassing the worker compensation laws of many other states.

lines.line: > Efforts to raise public awareness and gain legislative support were crucial. This included the roles of the governor, media journalists, and the tragic accident that spotlighted worker issues, leading to public and legislative backing.
** legislative support, public awareness, media, governor, tragic accident **

lines.line: # Bipartisan Efforts and Key Political Figures

> Republican involvement was key to legislative success, with individuals like Bill Deal playing pivotal roles. The bipartisan support and the efforts of various senators helped to push the bill forward, despite opposition.
** bipartisan, Republicans, Bill Deal, political support, opposition **

story.title: Johnson_Erik_OH 2004 Side B
story.description: Recorded on November 26, 1997. Topic is Johnson's work in the migrant farmworker unit of Idaho Legal Aid.
